data.title: Oil rallies over 2%; Brent at $108 amid Iran tensions, supply crunch
data.description:
Oil prices rose over 2% on Monday as U.S.-Iran peace talks stalled and shipments through the Strait of Hormuz remained constrained, keeping global supplies tight. Brent is up 2.5% to $108.05/bbl in early-morning trading, after rallying almost 17% over the course of last week.

U.S. crude (CL1:COM) rose 2% to $96.36 at press time.

"The lack of progress means the market is tightening every day, requiring oil prices to reprice at higher levels," ING analysts said in a note.

"There’s little alternative to fill a roughly 13m b/d shortfall. In the short term, inventories help to fill the gap, whether commercial or strategic reserves. Clearly, the longer this persists, the more demand destruction we will need to see. To see further demand destruction, prices will need to move higher."

However, there are reports this morning that Iran has offered the US a new proposal to reopen the Strait of Hormuz while delaying nuclear negotiations for a later stage. "US efforts to cut off Iranian oil would add to the upside," the analysts said.

The US also has tightened Iranian oil-related sanctions. It imposed sanctions on China’s Hengli Petrochemical (Dalian) Refinery Co. for its purchases of Iranian oil, as well as approximately 40 shipping companies and vessels that form part of Iran’s shadow fleet.

Meanwhile, Goldman Sachs increased its fourth-quarter oil price forecasts to $90 per barrel for Brent crude (CO1:COM) and $83 for West Texas Intermediate (CL1:COM), citing reduced output from the Middle East.

Elsewhere, President Donald Trump is reportedly expected to hold a Situation Room meeting on Iran on Monday with his top national security and foreign policy team.

data.title: Biggest stock movers Monday: OGN, QCOM, and more
data.description:
Stock index futures edged lower Monday morning as investors balanced cautious optimism ahead of this week's Big Tech earnings bonanza against ongoing uncertainty from the U.S.-Iran conflict. With five Magnificent Seven giants on deck and oil elevated, the market is primed for one of its most defining weeks of the year.

Here are some of Monday's biggest stock movers:

Biggest stock gainers

  • Intellia Therapeutics (NTLA) +25% - Shares surged as anticipation builds for its upcoming Phase 3 HAELO clinical trial results of lonvoguran ziclumeran (lonvo-z) in the treatment of hereditary angioedema ((HAE)). Investors are positioning themselves for potentially positive topline results from the HAE program. This significant movement in the stock price reflects growing expectations surrounding its in vivo CRISPR-based therapy and the wider gene-editing pipeline, with the upcoming data readout viewed as a crucial catalyst for the stock's performance.
  • Organon (OGN) +15% - Shares surged after Sun Pharmaceutical Industries agreed to acquire the company in an all-cash deal valuing it at ~$11.75B enterprise value, with shareholders set to receive $14 per share. The transaction, expected to close in early 2027, strengthens Sun Pharma’s global footprint and expands its presence in women’s health, biosimilars, and developed markets, while creating a combined entity with ~$12.4B in revenue.
  • Qualcomm (QCOM) +10% - Shares gained after TF International Securities analyst Ming-Chi Kuo indicated that OpenAI is exploring partnerships with Qualcomm and MediaTek to develop smartphone processors, with Luxshare Precision Industry as the exclusive co-design and manufacturing partner. While mass production is not expected until 2028, the potential collaboration points to longer-term demand tailwinds from AI-driven hardware upgrade cycles, supporting sentiment around Qualcomm’s positioning in next-gen mobile chips.
  • Compass Therapeutics (CMPX) +6% - Shares rose ahead of a key catalyst, as the company is set to present topline secondary endpoint data from its Phase 2/3 COMPANION-002 study evaluating tovecimig in advanced biliary tract cancer. The upcoming webcast is driving investor anticipation around clinical outcomes and potential efficacy signals for its bispecific antibody program.

Biggest stock losers

  • GE Vernova (GEV) -2% - Shares fell after BNP Paribas downgraded the stock to Neutral, citing limited visibility on sustaining growth as approximately 90% of gas turbine capacity is already contracted through 2030. The firm flagged that further upside would require incremental order momentum from projects not yet approved, while longer-term growth tied to AI-driven power demand remains uncertain. Although not a bearish call, the downgrade reflects concerns around execution risk and constrained capacity expansion, with a slightly lower price target of $1,190.

data.title: Wall Financial reports Q4 results
data.description:
• Wall Financial (WFICF) (WFC:CA) Q4 press release GAAP EPS of $0.10.
  • Revenue of $36.09M (-16.8% Y/Y).

data.title: Airlines’ hedging gaps heighten fuel risk as Iran tensions persist
data.description:
Airlines globally remain unevenly protected against jet fuel price shocks, leaving parts of the industry exposed as geopolitical risks around Iran threaten supply chains.

Data from Société Générale showed wide divergence in hedging strategies. In Europe, carriers such as Ryanair (RYAAY) and easyJet (ESYJY) have among the highest disclosed hedge ratios, often covering 80% or more of near-term fuel needs.

Lufthansa Group (DLAKY) also maintained extensive rolling hedges, though it remains exposed to refining margin swings. Air France-KLM (AFRAF) has stepped up hedging into 2026, signaling growing caution.

By contrast, several airlines have limited or no systematic hedging. SAS stood out with zero hedging, while U.S. carriers, including American Airlines (AAL), United Airlines (UAL), Delta Air Lines (DAL) and JetBlue Airways (JBLU) largely report no formal hedge positions, increasing sensitivity to spot fuel prices.

Southwest Airlines (LUV) remains a partial exception with limited hedging activity.

In Asia-Pacific, Qantas Group (QUBSF) and Virgin Australia show high hedge ratios, but their reliance on crude-linked instruments leaves them exposed to refining margins.

Singapore Airlines (SINGY) has one of the longest disclosed hedge horizons, while Cathay Pacific (CPCAY) relies primarily on crude options without crack spread protection.

Rising tensions between Washington and Tehran were already feeding into the fuel markets, with traders watching any disruption risk around the Strait of Hormuz. Even the threat of tighter flows has pushed jet fuel costs higher, leaving poorly hedged airlines more exposed to sudden price spikes.

data.title: Google to expand AI footprint with Seoul campus initiative
data.description:
South Korea and Google (GOOGL) have agreed to establish an AI campus in Seoul to strengthen collaboration between the tech giant and local engineers and startups, according to presidential policy adviser Kim Yong-beom.

The “AI Campus” will be housed within Google’s (GOOGL) existing Seoul offices and will function as a hub for Korean universities, research institutes, and startups to collaborate with Google DeepMind’s AI researchers.

Google DeepMind CEO Demis Hassabis and South Korea’s Deputy Prime Minister and Science and ICT Minister Bae Kyung-Hoon signed an MOU for the project.

As part of the discussions, South Korea requested that Google (GOOG) deploy at least 10 engineers from its U.S. headquarters to the Seoul campus. While Hassabis hopes the partnership will "help with training up the next generation in these amazing technologies through internships at our AI hub and other training programmes."

Describing South Korea as a “great industrial base,” Hassabis pointed to the country’s strengths across critical AI value chains, including semiconductors and robotics, positioning it as a strategic partner for next-generation innovation.

The partnership comes amid rising concerns about AI-driven job disruption, with President Lee raising the need to consider a base wage framework to mitigate potential employment losses linked to automation.

data.title: Iran's foreign minister to meet Putin in Russia as U.S. talks stall
data.description:
Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi has arrived in Russia, where he is expected to meet with President Vladimir Putin, even as negotiations with the ​U.S. reached a stalemate over the weekend.

Araghchi's visit aims "to coordinate cooperation and advance joint programs at both the regional and international levels," Kazem Jalali, Iran's ambassador to Russia, posted on X. 

"Iran and Russia are present in a united front in the campaign of the world's totalitarian forces against independent and justice-seeking countries, as well as countries that seek a world free from unilateralism and Western domination," he added.

Araghchi had traveled to Pakistan and Oman over the weekend ahead of his trip to Russia. He said he shared Iran's position on a workable framework to end the war, adding he has "yet to see if the U.S. is truly serious about diplomacy."

The minister's discussions with Omani officials focused on ways to ensure safe transit through the Strait of Hormuz.

Iran reportedly presented a new proposal to the U.S. to reopen the strait, while postponing nuclear talks amid division within the Iranian leadership over what concessions should be on the table.

"If they want to talk, they can come to us, or they can call us," President Donald Trump told Fox News on Sunday. "They know what has to be in the ⁠agreement. It's very simple: They cannot have a nuclear weapon; otherwise, there's no reason to meet."

Trump canceled a planned trip of his envoys Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner to Pakistan, saying it would be a waste of time given Iran's inadequate offer. 

The president is expected to hold a Situation Room meeting on Iran on Monday to discuss the stalemate in negotiations and potential options for the next steps in the war, Axios reported.

data.title: Bitmine files for resale of 501,545 shares issued in Pier Two deal
data.description:
• Bitmine (BMNR) in its regulartory filing registers a potential resale of its 501,545 shares issued in its Pier Two acquisition.
  • The crypto mining and services company said it was not selling any shares in the offering and will not receive any proceeds from any sales by the stockholders.
  • The shares were issued as initial stock consideration for Bitmine’s purchase of Pier Two.
  • The selling stockholders, or their transferees, may sell the shares from time to time in public or private transactions at market or negotiated prices, said Bitmine

data.title: Agree Realty starts $1.75B ATM share offering
data.description:
Agree Realty (ADC) started at-the-market equity offering to sell up to $1.75B of common stock from time to time, the company said in its regulatory filing on Friday.

The retail-focused REIT said the agreement includes a syndicate of banks that includes Wells Fargo, Baird, Bank of America, BTIG, Citigroup, Evercore, Jefferies, JP Morgan, Mizuho, and Morgan Stanley, among others.

Sales may be executed through negotiated transactions, block trades, or open-market offerings on the New York Stock Exchange.

In addition to selling common stock through its ATM program, Agree Realty has entered into forward sale agreements with multiple banks, allowing the company to raise capital later through physical settlement, or elect cash or net-share settlement alternatives.

The company said it would not initially receive proceeds from borrowed-share sales tied to those transactions.

The company simultaneously terminated its prior at-the-market program established in October 2024.

data.title: China blocks Meta’s $2B takeover of AI firm Manus: report
data.description:
• China has reportedly decided to block Meta Platform’s (META) $2B acquisition of agentic AI startup Manus.
  • The National Development and Reform Commission ordered the deal’s cancellation in a brief statement Monday, as per reports.
  • The decision was made in accordance with laws and regulations, the powerful state planner said in a one-line notice, without elaborating, Bloomberg reported.

data.title: Pony AI launches new autonomous driving controller built on NVIDIA tech for L4 robotaxis and mobility use
data.description:
• Pony AI (PONY) has unveiled a next-generation autonomous driving domain controller built on NVIDIA DRIVE (NVDA) Hyperion platform and powered by DRIVE AGX Thor with NVLink, aimed at L4 robotaxis and broader autonomous mobility applications.
  • The new system is designed to deliver significant gains in AI computing performance, energy efficiency and support for the latest AI models, while meeting core L4 requirements such as multi-sensor fusion, full-scenario perception and high-complexity scenario understanding, the company said. 
  • Pony.ai expects the new platform to support a portfolio spanning multiple compute tiers and cooling solutions, enabling deployment across a wide range of autonomous applications. Pony.ai has been a long-standing NVIDIA partner since 2017.
